text: This video will illustrate a robotic left lower lobectomy in a patient who was a 59 year old female with a left lower lobe adenocarcinoma, approximately 2.8 centimeters in size.
The dissection is carried out by mobilizing the inferior pulmonary ligament as seen here to the level of the inferior pulmonary vein.
The lung is retracted anteriorly to expose the posterior aspect of the hilum
Here's some level 10 lymph nodes are seen and encountered and removed.
That white structure there is the pulmonary artery.
Again, some more lymph nodes here that are encountered and these will also be removed and sent for pathology
The lung is then folded down towards the diaphragm and is exposed to the AP window space.
In this case, there are no AP window lymph nodes seen.
However, there is this level 10 lymph node anteriorly that is removed and sent for analysis.
After the lymphadenectomy portion of the operation, we turn our attention to performing the actual lobectomy.
Here the pulmonary vein is seen is dissected out circumferentially and then transected with the vascular load of the stapler.
Dissection is then started out in the fissure.
In this case, the pulmonary artery was readily seen in the fissure.
The pleura overlying it is exposed.
Here there's a lymph node seen and this is also removed and sent for pathology.
The posterior fissure is then completed using a green load of the stapler.
This is then followed by performing the exact same maneuver to finish the anterior fissure.
Here again, a big lymph is encountered and this is removed.
Again a green load is used here to transect the anterior fissure.
Next the pulmonary artery is then dissected.
Here you can see the dissection between the pulmonary artery and the bronchus posteriorly.
And once completely on the artery, we'll go ahead and use a vascular load to transect it.
Lastly, the bronchus to the left lower lobe is encircled and transected with a gluing load of the stapler.
And this completes the left lower lobectomy in this case.
In all of our cases, we do perform an intercostal nerve block, which you see here, followed by application of Pro-gel.
This patient did very well postoperatively and was sent home the next day.
